topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

The small, awkwardly wrapped box sat on my desk, a jarring splash of bright, unnecessary color against the beige monotony of my workspace. I stared at it, my jaw tightening until a dull ache radiated through my temples. Liam. He hadn't spoken to me since graduation, a cold silence that had been building for months. Now, this. I picked up the ribbon, my fingers brushing against the cheap paper. A sharp, unpleasant feeling coiled low in my stomach. I hadn't asked for anything. I certainly hadn't expected anything from him. When I finally tore the paper open, the contents—a meticulously crafted, antique fountain pen—looked utterly ridiculous. I let out a short, sharp exhale, the sound catching in my throat. I pushed the box away with unnecessary force, the scrape of the cardboard against the wood echoing too loudly in the quiet classroom.

[story 2]

I watched the delivery slip under the door, my knuckles white as I gripped the edge of my worn oak desk. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring. It was Mark, one of the students I’d struggled to connect with during my tenure. He’d sent this. A heavy, leather-bound journal. The sheer audacity of it made my chest feel tight, constricted and shallow. I didn't move toward the door immediately. Instead, I paced the small perimeter of my office, my steps quick and uneven. "What is this?" My voice came out brittle, pitched higher than I intended. When I finally opened it, the scent of aged paper hit me, and a wave of profound irritation washed over me. I slammed the journal shut, the sound echoing in the sudden stillness. I didn't even look at the inscription; I just wanted it gone.

[story 3]

The small, velvet-lined presentation case rested on my filing cabinet, mocking me with its polished gleam. Sarah. She was the one who always seemed to glide through my lectures, effortlessly brilliant and utterly detached. I felt a hot flush creep up my neck, making the skin prickle unpleasantly. It was a silver locket, delicate and undeniably expensive. I picked it up, turning it over in my palm, feeling the cool metal against my skin. A tremor ran through my hand, and I quickly dropped it back onto the cabinet with a decisive clatter. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly across my chest, my gaze fixed on the window. The air in the room felt thick, heavy with unspoken resentment. I wanted to look away, to erase the sight of that gift entirely.
